content: The distance travelled by an object along a straight line in time t is given by $$s = 3 - 4t + 5{t^2}$$, the initial velocity of the object is
Acceleration-time $(a-t)$ graph of a body is shown in the figurd. Corresponding velocity-time $(v-t)$ graph is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text {}\\ As, \,\,\,&\frac{d v}{d t}=\tan \theta=a \end{aligned} $$

content: Ruma reached the metro station and found that the escalator was not working. She walked up the stationary escalator with velocity $v_1$ in time $t_1$. On other day if she remains stationary on the escalator moving with velocity $v_2$, then escalator takes her up in time $t_2$. The time taken by her to walk up with velocity $v_1$ on the moving escalator will be
explanation:
Let the escalator have “length’’ $L$ (in steps).

Walking alone (escalator off):

$v_1\,t_1=L$.

Standing alone (escalator on):

$v_2\,t_2=L$.

Both walking and moving: net speed $v_1+v_2$, so time

$ t=\frac{L}{v_1+v_2} =\frac{v_1\,t_1}{v_1+v_2} =\frac{\tfrac{L}{t_1}\,t_1}{\tfrac{L}{t_1}+\tfrac{L}{t_2}} =\frac{L}{L\bigl(\tfrac1{t_1}+\tfrac1{t_2}\bigr)} =\frac{1}{\tfrac1{t_1}+\tfrac1{t_2}} =\frac{t_1\,t_2}{t_1+t_2}\,. $

Answer: $\displaystyle \frac{t_1\,t_2}{t_1+t_2}$ (Option B).

content: The acceleration-time graph of a particle moving in a straight line is shown in the figure. If the initial velocity of the particle is zero then the velocity-time graph of the particle will be
explanation:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (a) } 0 \leq \text { t } \leq \frac{\mathrm{T}}{2} \\\\ & \frac{d f}{d t}=1 \\\\ & f=t \\\\ & \frac{d v}{d t}=t \\\\ & v=\frac{t^2}{2} \text { parabola ( } v \text { is axis) } \\\\ & (v)_{\frac{T}{2}}=\frac{T^2}{8} \\\\ & \end{aligned}$

(b)

$\begin{aligned} \frac{T}{2} & \leq t \leq T \\\\ \frac{\mathrm{df}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =-1\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & f=-t+C_1 \\\\ & \frac{T}{2}=-\frac{T}{2}+C_1 \\\\ & C_1=T \\\\ & f=T-t \\\\ & V=T t-\frac{t^2}{2}+C_2 \\\\ & \frac{T^2}{8}=\frac{T^2}{2}-\frac{T^2}{8}+C_2 \end{aligned}$

$$ \mathrm{C}_2=\frac{\mathrm{T}^2}{4}-\frac{\mathrm{T}^2}{2}=-\frac{\mathrm{T}^2}{4} $$

$\therefore V=T t=\frac{t^2}{2}-\frac{T^2}{4}$

parabola (axis is parallel to $v$ and downward)

content: The acceleration versus distance graph for a particle moving with initial velocity 5 m/s is shown in the figure. The velocity of the particle at x = 35 m will be
explanation:
During first half of the motion (i.e. upto x = 20 m) acceleration in increasing linearly is given by

$$a = {5 \over {20}}x + 5 \Rightarrow a = {x \over 4} + 5$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dv} \over {dt}} = {x \over 4} + 5$$ $$\because$$ $$\left( {a = {{dv} \over {dt}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{v\,.\,dt} \over {dx}} = {x \over 4} + 5$$ $$\because$$ $$\left( {{{dx} \over {dt}} = v \Rightarrow dt = {{dx} \over v}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int\limits_5^v {v\,.\,dv = \int\limits_0^{20} {\left( {{x \over 4} + 5} \right)\,.\,dx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {{{{v^2}} \over 2}} \right]_5^v = \left[ {{{{x^2}} \over 8} + 5x} \right]_0^2$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{v^2}} \over 5} - {{25} \over 2} = {{400} \over 8} + 100$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{v^2}} \over 2} = 150 + {{25} \over 2} \Rightarrow {v^2} = 325$$

Now, in second half of motion, acceleration is constant, i.e. a = 10 m/s2

$$\Rightarrow$$ v2 $$-$$ u2 = 2as

$$\Rightarrow$$ v'2 $$-$$ v2 = 2as (take, v = v', u = v)

$$\Rightarrow$$ v'2 $$-$$ 325 = 2 $$\times$$ 10 $$\times$$ (35 $$-$$ 20) ($$\because$$ v2 = 325)

$$\Rightarrow$$ v' = $$\sqrt {625} $$ = 25 m/s

content: In case of projectile motion, which one of the following figures represent variation of horizontal component of velocity (u<sub>x</sub>) with time t? (Assume that air resistance is negligible)
explanation: In projectile motion, horizontal component at velocity remains constant with time. Hence, correct representation of graph between t and u<sub>x</sub> is shown in option (b).

content: A fighter plane, flying horizontally with a speed 360 km/h at an altitude of 500 m drops a bomb for a target straight ahead of it on the ground. The bomb should be dropped at what approximate distance ahead of the target? Assume that acceleration due to gravity (g) is 10 ms<sup>-2</sup>. Also, neglect air drag.
explanation:
Given, speed of fighter plane, v = 360 km/h

= $$360 \times {5 \over {18}}$$ m/s = 100 m/s

Altitude, h = 500 m


Now, from equation of motion,

$$h = ut + {1 \over 2}g{t^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$500 = 0(t) + {1 \over 2} \times 10 \times {t^2}$$ ........$$\left[ {\matrix{ {u = 0and} \cr {g = 10m/{s^2}} \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ t2 = 100 $$ \Rightarrow $$ t = 10 s

$$ \therefore $$ The bomb should be dropped at the distance,

x = vt = 100 $$ \times $$ 10 = 1000 m

content: Two particles are simultaneously projected in the horizontal direction from a point P at a certain height. The initial velocities of the particles are oppositely directed to each other and have magnitude v each. The separation between the particles at a time when their position vectors (drawn from the point P) are mutually perpendicular, is
explanation:
According to the question,


In two dimension, the position vectors r1 and r2 represented as

$${r_1} = vt\widehat i - {1 \over 2}g{t^2}\widehat j$$ ...(i)

$${r_2} = vt( - \widehat i) - {1 \over 2}g{t^2}(\widehat j)$$ ... (ii)

$$ \because $$ we know that, when the two vectors are mutually perpendicular, i.e.

$$\theta = 90^\circ $$

So, $${r_1}\,.\,{r_2} = {r_1}{r_2}\cos 90^\circ $$

$${r_1}\,.\,{r_2} = 0$$

Substituting the values r1 and r2 in the above relation, we get

$$((vt)\widehat i - {1 \over 2}g{t^2}\widehat j)\,.\,(vt( - \widehat i) - {1 \over 2}g{t^2}(\widehat j)) = 0$$

$$ - {v^2}{t^2} + {1 \over 4}4{g^2}{t^4} = 0$$ (where, $$\widehat i\,.\,\widehat i = \widehat j\,.\,\widehat j = \widehat k\,.\,\widehat k = 1$$)

$${v^2}{t^2} = {1 \over 4}{g^2}{t^4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {v^2} = {1 \over 4}{g^2}{t^2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Magnitude of velocity of the particles, $$v = {1 \over 2}gt$$

We know that, separation distance between particles at a time t

$$\Delta x = 2vt$$

$$\Delta x = 2 \times v \times {{2v} \over g} \Rightarrow \Delta x = {{4{v^2}} \over g}$$

content: At a particular height, the velocity of an ascending body is u. The velocity at the same height while the body falls freely is
explanation:
Given that velocity of ascending body = u



Let there is no air resistance when body falls freely in downward direction, the direction of falling body will opposite to the ascending body.

$$ \therefore $$ The velocity at the same height while the body falls freely = $$-$$ u

content: A projectile thrown with an initial velocity of 10 ms<sup>$$-$$1</sup> at an angle $$\alpha$$ with the horizontal, has a range of 5 m. Taking g = 10 ms<sup>$$-$$2</sup> and neglecting air resistance, what will be the estimated value of $$\alpha$$?
explanation:
Given, initial velocity of particle, u = 10 ms$$-$$1

Range, R = 5 m

Gravitational acceleration, g = 10 ms$$-$$2

As we known that,

Range of projectile, $$R = {{{u^2}\sin 2\theta } \over g}$$

$$5 = {{{{(10)}^2}\sin 2\alpha } \over {10}}$$

$$\sin 2\alpha = {5 \over {10}}$$

$$\sin 2\alpha = {1 \over 2}$$

$$\alpha = 15^\circ $$

or $$(90^\circ - 15^\circ = 75^\circ )$$

content: A train moves from rest with acceleration $$\alpha$$ and in time t<sub>1</sub> covers a distance x. It then decelerates to rest at constant retardation $$\beta$$ for distance y in time t<sub>2</sub>. Then,
explanation:
Shape of the graph OP shows the acceleration in train

$$ \therefore $$ tan$$\theta$$ = acceleration.

$$\alpha = {{{v_0}} \over {{t_1}}}$$

Shape of the graph pt shows the deceleration in train.

$$ \therefore $$ Similarly, $$\beta = {{{v_0}} \over {{t_2}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\beta \over \alpha } = {{{v_0}/{t_2}} \over {{v_0}/{t_1}}} = {{{t_1}} \over {{t_2}}}$$

Displacement = area of graph obtained between v - t

$$x = {1 \over 2}{t_1}.{v_0}$$

$$y = {1 \over 2}{t_2}.{v_0}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${x \over y} = {{{t_1}} \over {{t_2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x \over y} = {{{t_1}} \over {{t_2}}} = {\beta \over \alpha }$$
